subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, IDB, Central Bank of Belize, Freedom House, RSF, InSight Crime) + international and Belizean press.
A stable democracy with a striking debt turnaround - the 2021 'blue bond' debt-for-nature swap cut public debt from ~103% of GDP (2020) to ~61% (2024), with the IMF targeting 50% by FY2030.

Standout features: an improving fiscal/governance profile and a free press, against thin reserves, correspondent-banking de-risking, hurricane exposure, elevated (falling) crime, and the unresolved Guatemala claim at the ICJ.

economic.notes: A striking debt turnaround and low inflation on a USD peg, against thin reserves and bank de-risking.

fiscal_state.notes: Debt cut to ~61% of GDP (2024) from 103% (2020); the IMF endorses a path to 50% by FY2030 but flags slow consolidation and a post-2032 blue-loan repayment cliff. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The BZD is pegged 2:1 to the USD since 1976 - no independent monetary policy, the peg is the primary mandate. (Central Bank of Belize)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The BZD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Moderated sharply to ~1.0% (May 2025) from ~4% a year earlier. (IMF; Central Bank of Belize)

banking_stability.notes: Domestic banks broadly stable, but acute correspondent-banking de-risking (banks lost ~83% of CBRs 2013-16) remains a structural fragility for trade/tourism payments. (IDB; CSIS)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A very small, open, tourism/agriculture (sugar, citrus)-dependent economy; thin reserves limit shock-absorption and any trade leverage. (IMF 2025)

institutional.notes: Functioning democratic institutions and a free press, with corruption and impunity for violent crime.

rule_of_law.notes: Functioning courts and democratic norms (Freedom House 'Free', 87/100), but corruption and impunity for violent crime are noted weaknesses. (Freedom House)

statistical_integrity.notes: Engages credibly with the IMF/World Bank Article IV process, but a small statistical apparatus and data gaps warrant lower confidence. (IMF)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Limited administrative depth typical of a ~410k-population state; reform-implementation capacity is a recurring IMF concern. (IMF)

press_freedom.notes: RSF 2025 ranked Belize 47/180 - a pluralistic, generally free media environment. (RSF)

political_social.notes: A regular peaceful-rotation democracy in a diverse, broadly peaceful society with localized gang violence.

political_stability.notes: PM Briceno/PUP re-elected decisively (26 seats) in March 2025, with regular peaceful power rotation, US/OAS-endorsed. (National Assembly; US State Dept)

civil_liberties.notes: Mostly respected (Freedom House civil liberties 53/60), with police-conduct and crime-related concerns. (Freedom House 2025)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts Central American migrants/refugees - generally tolerant but capacity-constrained. (lower confidence)

social_cohesion.notes: Diverse (Creole, Mestizo, Maya, Garifuna) and broadly peaceful, with gang violence localized in Belize City. (InSight Crime)

demographics.notes: A young, growing population - a long-run asset if jobs/education keep pace, favorable vs aging peers. (World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: Stable Western/CARICOM alignment, with the Guatemala claim a low-probability high-tail-risk overhang.

alliance_reliability.notes: A stable Western/Commonwealth/CARICOM-aligned UK/US partner pursuing ICJ resolution diplomatically. (observed)

conflict_involvement.notes: No active conflict; the Guatemala claim is being arbitrated peacefully at the ICJ (President Arevalo pledged to honor the ruling) - an unresolved sovereignty tail. (ICJ)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Not sanctioned; the main external-finance vulnerability is bank de-risking rather than capital controls. (IDB/CSIS)

physical_practical.notes: High hurricane/flood exposure and elevated (falling) gang crime, with improving but gappy services.

climate.notes: Low coastal cays plus reef dependence plus a dry-corridor edge, high vulnerability. (WB CCDR)

healthcare.notes: A basic public system with limited specialist capacity, typical of a low-resource SIDS. (general)

infrastructure.notes: Improving (tourism-driven) but with transport/utility gaps the IMF flags as a priority. (IMF)

crime_safety.notes: Homicide ~21.7/100k (2024), among the higher regional rates though below the Northern Triangle and falling (~-29% H1 2025); concentrated and gang-driven. (Belize Crime Observatory; InSight Crime)

disaster_insurance.notes: CCRIF parametric coverage available regionally, but the protection gap and fiscal exposure to large storms remain significant. (CCRIF)

summary: A stable parliamentary democracy that has executed one of the region's most credible debt turnarounds, cutting public debt from over 100% to ~61% of GDP via its 2021 blue-bond debt-for-nature swap, with strong post-pandemic growth and inflation now near 1%. The principal risks are structural rather than political: thin foreign reserves (below the IMF's adequacy metric), heavy reliance on tourism and agriculture, acute correspondent-banking de-risking, and high hurricane exposure. Elevated but declining gang-driven crime in Belize City and the unresolved Guatemala territorial claim - now before the ICJ - are the standout tail-risks. Overall a moderately positive, improving profile constrained by small-state vulnerability.
